parsed_findings.chain_steps: `sort` is allowlisted, but `dir` remains attacker-controlled and unsanitized.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may alter query logic or run arbitrary SQL if the driver allows stacked statements.
parsed_findings.proof: `direction` comes from `request.args` and is inserted raw into the query at line 7.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `?sort=name&dir=asc;DROP TABLE users--` and the payload reaches SQL execution.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unvalidated `dir` is concatenated into the SQL `ORDER BY`, enabling SQL injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: The broken target check does not stop arbitrary URLs, so the fetch remains attacker-controlled.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can probe internal services and exfiltrate responses through the proxy.
parsed_findings.proof: `axios.get(u)` sends arbitrary attacker-supplied destinations from `req.query.url` directly.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:...` or another internal host and observe the proxy request it.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side without a positive allowlist, enabling SSRF.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use traversal to read sensitive files, then pivot with leaked credentials or keys.
parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary file read can expose secrets, configs, and source code.
parsed_findings.proof: Reqs like file=../../etc/passwd become ../etc/passwd after replace, then path.join resolves outside the base.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: GET /download?file=../../etc/passwd and observe the server attempt to read a file outside /srv/data.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Single replace only strips one ../, so crafted paths can still escape /srv/data.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Forge state -> hit /resume -> pickle executes payload before last_page is read.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution or full server compromise on request handling.
parsed_findings.proof: pickle.loads can invoke arbitrary object constructors from a forged base64 cookie.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send /resume with a crafted state cookie containing a malicious pickle payload.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Attacker-controlled cookie data is deserialized with pickle.loads, enabling code execution.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the IDOR to overwrite a privileged field, then authenticate as the modified account or elevate your own permissions.
parsed_findings.impact: Account takeover or privilege escalation via unauthorized profile and role changes.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 5 reads the record by `req.params.id`, then line 6 copies attacker-controlled `req.body` into it and line 7 persists it.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `PATCH /api/users/victim-id` with a body like `{"role":"admin"}` as a non-owner and the victim record is modified.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Any caller can patch any user by ID because the handler never verifies ownership before loading and saving the target record.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the injected shell to read secrets or pivot to full server compromise.
parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary command execution in the backup service context.
parsed_findings.proof: A value like `x.tgz;id` makes `os.system` execute `id` after `tar`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST JSON `{"name":"x.tgz;id","src":"/var/data/app"}` and observe the extra command run.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `name` is inserted into a shell command without quoting, enabling command injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the injected shell to exfiltrate files or escalate within the host.
parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary command execution despite the `/var/data/` allowlist.
parsed_findings.proof: A value like `/var/data/app;id` still passes `startswith` and breaks out of `tar`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST JSON `{"src":"/var/data/app;id","name":"daily"}` and confirm `id` executes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `src` is also passed unquoted to the shell, and the prefix check does not prevent shell metacharacters.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Post malicious Markdown, store rendered HTML, then trigger it when comments are displayed.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can run JavaScript in other users' browsers and steal sessions or perform actions.
parsed_findings.proof: `markdown.markdown()` preserves raw HTML, so a comment like `` is stored and later served.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST that payload to `/comments`, then load the comment list and execute the rendered HTML.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Stored XSS via unsanitized Markdown HTML rendering.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use negative transfer to inflate balance, then transfer the forged funds onward.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can create money from nothing and drain another account.
parsed_findings.proof: If `amt` is `-1`, line 8 adds 1 to the sender balance and line 9 subtracts 1 from the recipient.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `{"to":"victim","amount":-1}` as an authenticated user.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Negative `amount` is accepted and turns a transfer into balance minting for the sender.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Race multiple transfers against one balance snapshot to spend the same funds twice.
parsed_findings.impact: Concurrent requests can overdraw the account and violate ledger integrity.
parsed_findings.proof: Two concurrent requests can both read the same `src` before either write lands, so both pass line 6.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send two transfers with the same source balance at the same time.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The balance check and updates are not atomic, enabling double-spend races.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Trigger verify failure, hit the production fallback, and use the decoded payload as authenticated identity.
parsed_findings.impact: Authentication bypass and privilege escalation via attacker-controlled token claims.
parsed_findings.proof: The catch block returns jwt.decode(t) after any verify failure, so signature checks are skipped.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a forged or malformed Bearer JWT in production; if verify fails, the decoded payload is accepted.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Production falls back to unverified jwt.decode(), letting attackers supply trusted claims.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the timing leak to derive `want`, then submit a matching `X-Sig` header.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may forge webhook requests and trigger `process_event()` without knowing `SECRET`.
parsed_findings.proof: `got == want` short-circuits on the first mismatch instead of using a constant-time compare.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many signed requests with one guessed hex nibble changed and measure response timing deltas.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Using `==` to compare HMACs leaks timing differences and can let an attacker recover a valid signature.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the redirect to deliver a trusted-link phishing or token-leak chain.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can phish users, steal auth flows, or bounce victims through a trusted origin.
parsed_findings.proof: The slash-only check lets `//attacker.tld` pass, and `redirect()` will send the browser off-site.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/go?next=//evil.com` and observe the browser redirect to the attacker domain.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `next` allows protocol-relative URLs like `//evil.com`, creating an open redirect.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use operator injection to make `findOne` return a chosen account, then complete the login flow.
parsed_findings.impact: This can bypass authentication or authenticate as an unintended user.
parsed_findings.proof: If the body allows objects, an attacker can send `email[$ne]=x` or similar to alter `findOne(q)` matching.

parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `req.body` is used directly in a database filter, enabling NoSQL operator injection against login.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the fetch to reach localhost, admin panels, or metadata endpoints.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read internal services or pivot into the private network.

parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint is an SSRF sink because it fetches any `http` URL without an allowlist.

parsed_findings.impact: Stack corruption can crash the program or enable code execution.
parsed_findings.proof: gets() reads arbitrary-length input into char buf[64] with no bounds check.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Enter more than 63 bytes at the prompt.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unbounded gets(buf) can overflow the 64-byte stack buffer.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Pair this with device backup exposure or rooted-app file access to exfiltrate valid credentials.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker with app storage, backup, or root access can steal the token and hijack the session.
parsed_findings.proof: `putString('access_token', token)` persists the token unencrypted in app storage.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Trigger `saveToken()` then inspect the prefs file or backup to recover the token.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Access token is stored in plaintext SharedPreferences.

parsed_findings.impact: Confidentiality is lost and key rotation requires a code change.
parsed_findings.proof: `KEY` is a fixed literal on line 2, so the secret never changes and is trivially reusable.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Read line 2, copy the key, and use it to decrypt any `enc()` output with AES-CBC.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Hard-coded AES key lets anyone with source access decrypt or forge all ciphertexts.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Pass `out=NULL` and any nonzero `out_sz` to bypass the guard and hit the null write.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can trigger a process crash by supplying a NULL output pointer.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 3 returns only when `out` is non-NULL, then line 5 copies into `out` even when it is NULL.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name("x",NULL,2)` and the function reaches `memcpy` with a NULL destination.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The guard is inverted, so a NULL `out` pointer passes and gets dereferenced in `memcpy`.
